sections.header: Verses 1–8 — "I Have Fought the Good Fight"

heb.paragraph: The aorist imperative κήρυξον (keryxon) conveys urgency: proclaim the word now, without delay. The verb κηρύσσω (kerysso) denotes the herald's public proclamation of an official message—the preacher is not offering opinions but delivering the King's decree. This imperative, given 'in the presence of God and of Christ Jesus who will judge the living and the dead,' is the most solemn charge in the Pauline corpus.

heb.gloss: to be poured out as a drink offering
heb.paragraph: A sacrificial metaphor: Paul's life is being σπένδομαι (poured out as a libation). The drink offering (σπονδή) was the wine poured over a sacrifice as a final act of completion (cf. Phil 2:17, where Paul used the same metaphor earlier in his ministry). Here the pouring is already underway—Paul's execution is imminent.

refs.note: The charge to 'fight the good fight' finds its completion here: Timothy was charged to fight; Paul has fought and finished.
Paul's earlier use of the drink-offering metaphor was anticipatory; here it is realized—the pouring out has begun.
The race metaphor, used extensively in 1 Corinthians, reaches its climax here: Paul has finished the course.
In his farewell to the Ephesian elders, Paul declared his desire to finish the race and complete the task the Lord had given him—now accomplished.

notes.note: In the presence of God and of Christ Jesus, who will judge the living and the dead: preach the word. Be prepared in season and out of season. Correct, rebuke, and encourage—with great patience and careful instruction.

The time will come when people will not put up with sound doctrine. Instead, they will gather around them teachers who say what their itching ears want to hear. They will turn away from truth and turn aside to myths.
But you, keep your head in all situations, endure hardship, do the work of an evangelist, discharge all the duties of your ministry.

I am already being poured out like a drink offering. The time for my departure has come. I have fought the good fight, I have finished the race, I have kept the faith. Now there is in store for me the crown of righteousness, which the Lord, the righteous Judge, will award to me on that day—and not only to me but to all who have longed for his appearing.

Paul gives his most solemn charge: preach the word with urgency, for the day is coming when people will refuse sound doctrine and accumulate teachers who tell them what they want to hear. The minister must remain faithful regardless of the audience's receptivity.

Paul faces death with serene confidence: his life is a completed sacrifice, his race is finished, his faith is intact. The crown of righteousness awaits—not earned by merit but awarded by the righteous Judge to all who love his appearing. This is not boasting but testimony to God's sustaining grace.
The term ἀνάλυσις (departure) was used for loosing a ship from its moorings or striking a military camp—either metaphor conveys the transition from this life to the next.

The 'crown of righteousness' (στέφανος τῆς δικαιοσύνης) refers to the wreath awarded to victorious athletes. The genitive may be appositional ('the crown which is righteousness') or descriptive ('the crown awarded for righteous living').

The charge formula invokes both God and Christ as witnesses and the eschaton as the horizon: preaching is accountable to divine judgment. The 'itching ears' prophecy (v. 3) describes a market-driven approach to teaching where consumers determine content—the opposite of the herald model in which the King determines the message.

The three-fold declaration is the letter's emotional and theological apex. The αγών/δρόμος/πίστις (fight/race/faith) triad summarizes Paul's entire ministry in athletic-military terms. The στέφανος (crown/wreath) is the athlete's victory wreath, awarded by the δίκαιος κριτής (righteous judge)—a title that simultaneously evokes athletic judges and eschatological judgment.

The charge to preach is framed in the most solemn possible terms: before God, before Christ, in light of his appearing and his kingdom. The 'itching ears' prophecy (v. 3) describes not merely audience preference but a fundamental shift in the teacher-audience relationship: when consumers choose their own teachers, the prophetic function of preaching is destroyed.

Paul's farewell is constructed as a testament—the literary genre of a dying patriarch's final words (cf. Gen 49; Deut 33; Acts 20:17–35). The sacrificial imagery (σπένδομαι), athletic triumph (ἀγών, δρόμος), and eschatological hope (στέφανος) converge to present Paul's death as both completion and anticipation. The universalized promise ('all who have longed for his appearing') democratizes the apostolic hope: what awaits Paul awaits every faithful believer.

hist.context: Chapter 4 is widely regarded as one of the most moving passages in the NT. Paul's farewell (vv. 6–8) stands alongside the testaments of Jacob (Gen 49), Moses (Deut 33), Joshua (Josh 24), and Jesus (John 13–17) as a great deathbed charge. The athletic/military trifold in v. 7—'I have fought the good fight, I have finished the race, I have kept the faith'—creates an inclusio with 1 Tim 6:12, where Timothy was charged to 'fight the good fight.' What Paul charged Timothy to do, Paul has done. The 'crown of righteousness' (v. 8) awaits not Paul alone but 'all who have longed for his appearing'—a democratization of eschatological reward.

sections.header: Verses 9–22 — "Do Your Best to Come to Me Quickly"

heb.paragraph: In v. 17, Paul declares that the Lord 'stood at my side' (παρέστη μοι) and gave him strength at his first defense. The verb παρίστημι (paristemi) in the legal context means to stand beside as an advocate or supporter. Despite human desertion (v. 16, 'no one came to my support'), the Lord's presence ensured that the gospel message was fully proclaimed even in the Roman courtroom.

refs.note: Demas appears as a companion in these earlier letters; his desertion in 2 Tim 4:10 marks a tragic personal reversal.

Mark's earlier abandonment of Paul (during the first missionary journey) and Paul's subsequent refusal to take him on the second journey make Paul's request for Mark here (v. 11) a notable reconciliation.
Paul's declaration that he was 'delivered from the lion's mouth' (v. 17) draws on the Psalmic language of divine rescue from deadly danger.

notes.note: Do your best to come to me quickly. Demas has deserted me because he loved this world. Only Luke is with me. Get Mark and bring him with you, because he is helpful to me in my ministry.
I sent Tychicus to Ephesus. When you come, bring the cloak I left at Troas, and my scrolls—especially the parchments. Even facing death, Paul wanted his books.
Alexander the coppersmith did me a great deal of harm. The Lord will repay him. Be on your guard against him.

At my first defense, no one came to my support, but everyone deserted me. But the Lord stood at my side and gave me strength. He will rescue me from every evil attack and bring me safely to his heavenly kingdom. To him be glory for ever and ever.
Greet Priscilla and Aquila. Erastus stayed in Corinth. Trophimus I left sick in Miletus. Do your best to get here before winter. The Lord be with your spirit. Grace be with you all.

The personal details reveal a lonely apostle sustained by divine faithfulness alone. Demas's desertion is a warning: even close associates can fall away when the cost of discipleship becomes too high. Yet Paul harbors no bitterness toward those who failed him at his first defense—'may it not be held against them.' The Lord who stood at his side will bring him to his heavenly kingdom.

The closing greetings are not mere formalities but the threads of a community held together by love under pressure. Paul's urgency—'come before winter'—reflects both practical need (the cloak, the scrolls) and the paternal desire to see his spiritual son once more before death.
Mark's rehabilitation here—'he is useful to me'—contrasts with the rift in Acts 15:37–39 and represents one of the most significant personal reconciliations in the NT.

The 'parchments' (μεμβράνας) are likely animal-skin documents, possibly portions of the OT or Paul's own letters/notes. The 'scrolls' (βιβλία) may refer to papyrus rolls. The specificity of this request is considered strong evidence for authenticity.

The personal notices constitute the strongest argument for Pauline authorship: the specificity of names, places, and circumstances resists literary fabrication. Demas's trajectory (commended in Col 4:14/Phlm 24, deserter here) is narratively plausible. The reconciliation with Mark (v. 11) presupposes Acts 15:37–39 and demonstrates Pauline pastoral maturity. The parchment request (v. 13) reveals Paul as a lifelong learner even at death's door.

The first defense (πρώτη ἀπολογία) likely refers to the preliminary hearing (prima actio) in Roman legal procedure, after which Paul was remanded for a second hearing. The Lord's παρίστημι (standing beside) provides the divine advocacy that human friends failed to offer. The lion's-mouth deliverance (v. 17) is Psalmic language, not a reference to the arena.

The personal notices are less autobiographical addendum than pastoral strategy: they model both the costs (Demas's desertion) and the gifts (Mark's usefulness, Luke's loyalty) of gospel partnership. The cloak-and-parchments request humanizes the apostle and reinforces the letter's emphasis on embodied, material faith—a subtle rebuke to the opponents' spiritualizing tendencies.

The final section balances human abandonment with divine faithfulness: 'no one stood with me... but the Lord stood at my side.' This is the letter's pastoral climax: even when every human support fails, divine presence sustains the faithful minister. The benediction ('the Lord be with your spirit; grace be with you all') extends from Timothy individually to the community corporately—the singular 'you' becomes plural, revealing that this 'personal' letter always had a congregational audience.

hist.context: The personal notices in vv. 9–21 are the strongest evidence for Pauline authorship of 2 Timothy: the specificity of names, places, and requests is difficult to explain as literary fiction. Demas, who abandoned Paul 'because he loved this world' (v. 10), was previously commended in Col 4:14 and Phlm 24—a sad trajectory. Alexander the coppersmith (v. 14) may be the same figure mentioned in 1 Tim 1:20. Luke alone remaining with Paul (v. 11) accords with the 'we' passages in Acts. The request for the cloak and scrolls left at Troas (v. 13) provides a poignant domestic detail: even facing death, Paul wanted his books.

rows.label: vv.1–5
rows.text: Solemn charge to preach the word

rows.label: vv.6–8
rows.text: Paul’s farewell: fight, race, faith, crown

rows.label: vv.16–18
rows.text: Divine faithfulness at the first defense

lit.note: Chapter 4 is Paul’s testament: the solemn charge to preach (vv. 1–5), the serene farewell (vv. 6–8), the human details of loyalty and betrayal (vv. 9–18), and the final greetings (vv. 19–22). The chapter moves from cosmic commission to intimate request, holding together the grandeur and the vulnerability of apostolic ministry.

themes.note: Mission peaks with the solemn charge to preach. Faith culminates in Paul’s farewell declaration. Sovereignty and Judgment frame the eschatological horizon of Christ’s appearing. Mercy appears in Paul’s forgiveness of those who deserted him.

hebtext.note: The aorist imperative κήρυξον (keryxon) conveys urgency: proclaim the word now, without delay. The verb κηρύσσω (kerysso) denotes the herald's public proclamation of an official message—the preacher is not offering opinions but delivering the King's decree. This imperative, given 'in the presence of God and of Christ Jesus who will judge the living and the dead,' is the most solemn charge in the Pauline corpus.

Phil 2:17, where Paul used the same metaphor earlier in his ministry). Here the pouring is already underway—Paul's execution is imminent.

In v. 17, Paul declares that the Lord 'stood at my side' (παρέστη μοι) and gave him strength at his first defense. The verb παρίστημι (paristemi) in the legal context means to stand beside as an advocate or supporter. ppl.name: Paul
ppl.role: Apostle to the Gentiles; author of thirteen NT letters
ppl.text: Paul (Saul of Tarsus) is introduced as a persecutor at Stephen’s stoning (Acts 7:58) and is dramatically converted on the Damascus road (Acts 9). He dominates Acts 13–28 across three missionary journeys, planting churches from Cyprus to Greece to Rome. His final journey to Rome as a prisoner frames Acts’ theological climax: the gospel reaching the empire’s capital, "boldly and without hindrance" (Acts 28:31).

ppl.name: Timothy
ppl.role: Paul’s young protégé; co-author of six NT letters
ppl.text: Timothy is recruited at Lystra on the second journey (Acts 16:1–3) — the son of a Jewish mother and Greek father, circumcised by Paul to aid Jewish outreach. He becomes Paul’s most trusted emissary, sent to troubled churches when Paul cannot go. Paul twice calls him "my true son in the faith" and addresses two personal letters to him. He is co-author of six Pauline letters and appears at the end of Hebrews.

ppl.name: Demas
ppl.text: Demas, who abandoned Paul 'because he loved this world' (v…

ppl.name: Luke
ppl.role: Physician; author of Luke-Acts; Paul’s companion
ppl.text: Luke the physician (Col 4:14) is identified by tradition as the author of Luke-Acts and as Paul’s companion in the "we" passages of Acts (16:10–17; 20:5–21:18; 27:1–28:16). He is with Paul during the second journey (Philippi onward), the voyage to Rome, and Paul’s imprisonment. Colossians 4:14 identifies him as "the beloved physician"; Philemon 24 calls him Paul’s fellow worker.

ppl.name: Mark
ppl.text: Get Mark and bring him with you, because he is helpful to me in my ministry…

ppl.name: Tychicus
ppl.text: Tychicus is a key figure in Paul’s Final Charge and Farewell.

ppl.name: Alexander the coppersmith
ppl.text: Alexander the coppersmith did me a great deal of harm…

ppl.name: Priscilla
ppl.role: Teacher and co-worker with Paul and Apollos
ppl.text: Priscilla (Prisca) and her husband Aquila are Jewish tentmakers expelled from Rome under Claudius. They meet Paul at Corinth (Acts 18:2) and join his team as both co-workers in tentmaking and teachers of the faith. When Apollos arrives at Ephesus with incomplete knowledge, Priscilla and Aquila "took him aside and explained to him the way of God more adequately" (Acts 18:26). She is mentioned before Aquila four of six times in the NT, suggesting her prominence.

rec.quote: "In the presence of God and of Christ Jesus, who will judge the living and the dead: preach the word. Be prepared in season and out of season. Correct, rebuke, and encourage—with great patience and car…"

rec.quote: "The term ἀνάλυσις (departure) was used for loosing a ship from its moorings or striking a military camp—either metaphor conveys the transition from this life to the next."

thread.text: The charge to 'fight the good fight' finds its completion here: Timothy was charged to fight; Paul has fought and finished.
Paul's earlier use of the drink-offering metaphor was anticipatory; here it is realized—the pouring out has begun.
The race metaphor, used extensively in 1 Corinthians, reaches its climax here: Paul has finished the course.
In his farewell to the Ephesian elders, Paul declared his desire to finish the race and complete the task the Lord had given him—now accomplished.
Demas appears as a companion in these earlier letters; his desertion in 2 Tim 4:10 marks a tragic personal reversal.

Mark's earlier abandonment of Paul (during the first missionary journey) and Paul's subsequent refusal to take him on the second journey make Paul's request for Mark here (v. 11) a notable reconciliation.

coaching.tip: 'I have fought the good fight, I have finished the race, I have kept the faith' (v. 7). Paul's summary: faithfulness, not fame. Three perfect tenses—completed action. The crown awaits (v. 8). His death approaches (v. 6); his confidence doesn't waver.

'Demas, because he loved this world, has deserted me' (v. 10). The same Demas commended in Colossians 4:14. Faithfulness falters; early promise doesn't guarantee perseverance. Paul writes alone—Luke remains (v. 11). Small remnants sustain.
